item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do wish there was just a bit more to go on here, maybe one more clue that could just subtly nudge the reader in the right direction, but what's here is really, really well done, and as a murder mystery junkie, I have to gladly upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Here's my headcanon:

+ Show
- Hide

"The fellow O5 murdered a colleague" ties up a lot of things except for all of the wonkiness that happened that day (The sight of a daylight comment, the undecipherable language bit, etc.) and I just think it's too clean and too mundane to say all of the evidence was fabricated by a rival O5, or even that an O5 used anomalous means to create the wonkiness as red herrings.

My headcanon is that TRAILHEAD did work, but not in the intended way. The specific language is that "O5-9 would only die as the result of a naturally-occurring, age-related illness." We're also told that it "ensured that O5-9 would meet a specific demise involving a gunshot(s) to the head." I think both are simultaneously true statements.

Maybe it was a mistake, and O5-9 didn't realize the second effect. Maybe he did, and he was attempting quantum immortality by ensuring two utterly incompatible future deaths. Either way, he was so sure that his little plan worked, he let his guard down. He had fewer security guards, fewer countermeasures, and no other anomalous protections. A would be-assassin actually made it into his dining room. But just as the assassin is about to pull the trigger, the universe goes wonky. Time speeds up. Local broadcast stations start playing messages from dozens of years in the future, in a yet-to-be-created language. Halley's Comet, not scheduled to arrive for another 75 years, appears in the sky. Individuals vanish and others skip ahead weeks or months into the future.

O5-9 is aged a lifetime in a few moments just as his assassin pulls the trigger. O5-9 dies of natural coronary failure, just as the bullets strike him. Both deaths happen simultaneously.

item.title: One problem
item.content:encoded: So while the theory is sound and does make a good case for how it is done, it doesn’t explain why O5-8 wanted to kill O5-9. though then again we aren’t given enough info to explain why he would do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Didn't like it, felt there was not enough payoff. I would expect to be able to piece together something more than what each O5 can know, since I can see all the information and they can't. Seems like an O5 could easily be fabricating this information just to kill another one. Probably 8, who erases the file without having been instructed to do so. But if I'm right, it's still too oblique.
Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why doesn't this have a higher rating?? I though it was brilliant. I particularly like the idea of splitting key pieces of information between different members of O-5 command, and the narrative use of quantum physics theories (could have been cheesy, but due to the expert use of technical tone, it wasn't).

+1, looking forward to reading more of your stuff :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rampant speculation
– hide block

O5-8 probably did it, as the "this document must be destroyed" was in O5-1's fragment of the description, but O5-8 was the one to delete it. O5-8 may have been trying to cover their tracks by fabricating TRAILHEAD so as to not give anyone enough conclusive information to point the murder back to him, then deleting it all as a supposed end of the world-preventing measure.

The big hole in this theory is the segment stating that a list of 15 suspects was compiled, but then again all of the agencies that compiled that list were not Foundation insiders. Said list probably did not include O5 Command, as I find it would be a major breach of security to let any of those agencies know enough about O5 to compile a list including any of them.

I feel like there was a massive missed potential in a "seven ate nine" joke with this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was my thought. It seems like the most likely scenario to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's worse than a murder mystery? A murder mystery you cannot solve under pain of universal collapse.

I really liked this one, as it took the murder mystery concept in a very thrilling direction, and reading the various bits of data at the end really built up the suspense.

+1 Kal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could it possibly be that O5-9 was killed by another O5, who subsequently set all this up to make sure nobody investigated the murder ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea of an O5 getting offed despite the existence of a Foundation project to make him immortal. However, something about the tone here felt off, like it was technically perfect to the point of being rather dry. You almost started to lose me until I got to the O5-1 note, which immediately brought the whole thing together and made me appreciate the preceding material a lot more. +1

Welcome back, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP has a really strong 'Cold War' vibe to it that I can't put my finger on; also, the idea of an anomaly that centers on a murder mystery that must not be solved is very cool.

I like this as it is, although I was also hoping for something in the fragmented records that indicated multiple narratives that are all simultaneously true (IE, O5-9 was killed by several people, and several methods, at several different times), with each O5 member being made aware of a different murder, and responsible for ensuring the evidence for this particular murder remains contained/does not interact with any evidence from the other variants (else we cause the emergence of destructive causal contradictions).

That being said, this took a much more low-key approach to the idea — one which I think also works really well.
